speaker: interviewer
text: You were telling me how in summer 1941, the Germans invaded Jaworow.

speaker: interviewee
text: Jaworow.

speaker: interviewer
text: Could you describe that day?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, it was a very horrible day. What we saw-- motorized armor on motorcycles. They came in with their sleeves up, up to here, and very powerful, very power. And the Russians-- the Russian tanks-- all laying in the streets and the highways-- Russian tank-- broken, all very bad. And they ran away, far away. And they-- just the officers ran away. Some of them couldn't even take their wives and children what they have. And that was really bad. They came in. First they went. And then other ones came in like the stormtroopers. They came in. And right away, they start getting to the Jews and take them to the synagogue. And they opened the doors. They took books, Jewish books, start burning them.

speaker: interviewer
text: You were there? You saw these?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, I didn't saw this. But that's what happened. Because they were hiding. Some of them were hiding. You know, they were afraid to go. But who they catch? They catched about 10 or 12 or 15-- something like that-- young, young Jews. Even my wife's friend, her husband, got killed the first, first time. He got killed. Then they shot them right there-- young people. They shot them. And then they start wandering around to, to grab Jews, take them to work. Women, they were taken also in the [INAUDIBLE].

speaker: interviewer
text: Where were you?

speaker: interviewee
text: To clean the windows and clean the walls, wash, and all of that stuff.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where were you while this was happening?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, I was by a friend, by a friend. Actually, not a friend-- this was like a family. My aunt, my aunt's brother, my aunt's husband's brother, he lived in Jaworow. And I was staying with him.

speaker: interviewer
text: His name?

speaker: interviewee
text: His name was Gavriil Danhirsch. Danhirsch was his name. I was staying with him. And then they will start taking people that work. There was a farmer, also a landowner, was there and not far from, from Jaworow around the highway between Jaworow and Krakowice. There was the farmer. And over there, they took-- every day, they came the trucks and taken Jewish man, take them on the trucks. We didn't know where we going the first day. So they took us with the trucks going to that place. And--

speaker: interviewer
text: Could you describe how they caught you?

speaker: interviewee
text: How they caught me? They came into the house. And that's it. All the men-- they taked only the men, not old men.

speaker: interviewer
text: Who came to your house?

speaker: interviewee
text: The, the, the German, the Germans-- it was not Gestapo actually that time. There were just Germans. But they were making in in that place, they were fixing up the Russian some of tanks what they could fix or any armors or any artillery what they can fix up. So they took all the Jews. And they brought us there.

speaker: interviewer
text: How did they treat you when they came to your house?

speaker: interviewee
text: How they treated in the house-- they didn't have to treat us. They just took us. Rouse, rouse, rouse and put on trucks and go further, pick up from door to door. Filled up one truck. Filled up another truck. And that's the way we came.

speaker: interviewer
text: How did they know where the Jews were?

speaker: interviewee
text: How would they know? Oh, they had lot a, a lot a Gentiles, Ukrainians, where they showed where there are Jews, specially the neighborhood where the Jews were. There was no Gentiles living there. Most of the Jews lived together in one area-- and by the shul or in the middle of the town where all the Jewish businessmen were. So they actually didn't have to ask too much. But--

speaker: interviewer
text: How many Jews lived in Jaworow?

speaker: interviewee
text: Jaworow at that time was 3,000 Jews, Jaworow. That was about 15,000 population of Jaworow. It was a county, county of Jaworow. It was a nice city-- not big, but a nice city.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you see the synagogue, what it looked like?

speaker: interviewee
text: I saw. I saw the synagogue. Later, when have-- well, later, there was no synagogue no more. Couldn't go no more. It's all broken up inside, everything. Later on, there's a story about the synagogue. They were putting all the Jewish sick, sick people who has typhus. There was a lot of typhus going on. This was later on.

speaker: interviewer
text: So you can tell me later.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, that was later on.

speaker: interviewer
text: So now, you are taken away. And what happened?

speaker: interviewee
text: So we were taken. And they brought us in this place. And then they let everybody off. And then we have to go for miles and have chains and ropes pulling up tanks on the one side and on the other, and pulling tanks all by hand, not with tractors. They had tractors, but they want the Jews to do that. And pulling into this place, then go further, bring more armor-- all kind of armor. Everything was going on for months and months.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where did they take you?

speaker: interviewee
text: And they didn't even gave us to eat there-- just work a whole day. And later, cleaning up all of this what they could fix, cleaning up everything, shining up. And then they were put into order.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where were you?

speaker: interviewee
text: This was in [INAUDIBLE], the name-- not, [INAUDIBLE]-- Wielka. This was the name of the place was Wielka that the-- that farm. And this was going on for months and months and months.

speaker: interviewer
text: How many?

speaker: interviewee
text: And very hard-- they didn't give us no food. When we came home, we had a little food to eat. But otherwise, they didn't give.

speaker: interviewer
text: How many Jewish men were there?

speaker: interviewee
text: Couple hundred-- 200 to 300 people.

speaker: interviewer
text: And how many German officers were there?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, German officers, there was the head, the meister-- forgot his name. And then was a lot a, a lot a officer, a lot a soldiers, even soldiers, German soldiers.

speaker: interviewer
text: How many? Just plain uniform. Oh, I don't know-- about 20, 30, 20, 30. And some of them were engineers also, German engineers. But they were fixing up. They were all-- you know, they had machinery, you know, where to, to put together something, you know, to-- they had a lot a were engineers. How did they treat you?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, they didn't treat us good. Because they didn't give us enough to eat and just rush, rush, rush and screaming and hollering. And some of them even had whips. You know, a tank is not so easy to, to grab a tank. But it was on one side and the other side and some of them by hand pushing, you know? And this is soft. It's not like mud. It's not like on a highway.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were you ever hit by the whip?

speaker: interviewee
text: It was plenty of times.

speaker: interviewer
text: Could you describe that?

speaker: interviewee
text: Plenty of times-- I had on my, my arms, you know. Later it healed, you know. Had plenty of times. Also, later on, they were hitting us when they were putting us on the trucks, on trucks, putting us to go to a hard labor camp.

speaker: interviewer
text: We'll speak about it later.

speaker: interviewee
text: [INAUDIBLE] later.

speaker: interviewer
text: How did the treatment and being there affect you?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, you couldn't, you couldn't complain. So it affected everybody but couldn't complain. Couldn't say nothing. Couldn't say a word to them. But when there's no complaint, you can, can do nothing. And everybody was treated the same. You know, I was treated. There was a rich boy. There was a very rich businessman. Everybody was treated the same, rich or poor-- nobody, you know. Everybody was treated.

speaker: interviewer
text: So what happened then?

speaker: interviewee
text: So then, after months--

speaker: interviewer
text: How many months, sorry?

speaker: interviewee
text: This was till about '42-- till April '42, April '42. I also, be-- before that, before April '42, it started getting the real bad in the city. The farmers was afraid to come in with food. Because the police, you know, were hunting-- the German police were hunting and chasing.

speaker: interviewer
text: So and start getting-- everybody start getting hungry, no food. So what we did-- my wife actually, what she, she had a very-- people were trusting her, very goo trusting her. So they gave her materials. I'm sorry. We need to stop so that you can tell me about your wife when-- you said you have a wife?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. Yeah. Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: Could you describe when you met her and when she is part of the story?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, my wife, I met before the war. We supposed to go with my mother, because we were a very close family with her family. We were very close. We supposed to go to her wedding, 1935. She married in 1935.

speaker: interviewer
text: Your wife? What, what, what is her name?

speaker: interviewee
text: She married to a man from-- and his name was Schulem Egert, Schulem Egert-- E-G-E-R-T. My son, my stepson, goes on his name.

speaker: interviewer
text: And her name?

speaker: interviewee
text: Her name, her name was-- my wife's name, her name was Rachel, Ruth Rachel. She has a lot of names. [INAUDIBLE] they call it in Jewish, [INAUDIBLE]. So we supposed to go to the wedding. And the wedding was a storm. Was in the winter, was a bad storm. And we couldn't go from the village. We couldn't go to the wedding. And then after the wedding, later on when she was married already, she had an apartment. So my mother went with a gift to bring her a gift. In Europe, they used to give pots and pans and dishes, gifts-- no money, there was no such thing-- all kind of gifts. So my mother and me, we went to give her a gift.

speaker: interviewer
text: So then I saw her for the first time. Because I know, I know more about her husband. Because he was also-- his father was a, was a painter. And he learned my brother to be a painter. So I know him.

speaker: interviewee
text: But then I know her. And that's the way. And then I went away to Krakow. And then my mother moved away to Krakow. I went to the army. Till I saw her when I come back-- I saw her that time in Jaworow when I come back where my brother was there already. Because my mother send them out. And my brother-in-law was there. He was also a painter. But he u-- he used to work. They, they three of them-- oh, yeah. And my brother-in-law's brother was there also. He used to be the manager of my brother-in-law.

speaker: interviewer
text: And then what happened with her?

speaker: interviewee
text: So what's happened-- they were all in her apartment. They were all in her apartment, living in that apartment. So that's where I met my brother. And I asked my brother, where is my other brother? He say he's in [INAUDIBLE] by the family there.

speaker: interviewer
text: So it happen later on that they took her husband. They took him to the army. And they took a lot of other Jews from the city to the army. They didn't took me, because I was working in that magazine. I was working for the Russian government.

speaker: interviewee
text: I took over the job. Very good-- it worked out very good. I could give a lot of people food what I had, what I could spare for everybody. And I used-- and I-- I asked--

speaker: interviewer
text: Her hu-- her husband left. And what happened?

speaker: interviewee
text: Now, before her husband left, I want to say it was happened-- before that, it was happen like I start telling you. There was not enough food in the city. People were hungry. So people, they gave materials to my wife. People used to come from other, other cities-- Polish. They used to bring in all kind of food, bring it in and getting gold and diamonds, for material-- you know, like tobacco they used to bring in. And people wanted to smoke, smoke. You know, everybody was smoking.

speaker: interviewer
text: So what's happened, they used to bring. But till we get them, there was not enough food. So I say, let me go to the village. I'll go to the village. I'll take with me materials, a lot of materials. And I'm going to go to the village and collect some food. I can bring to the city. So I went. To who do I go first? First, I go to see a guy what he used to be our-- going with the horses, horse and wagon, with my mother, with me, with business, you know-- in business, helping us, you know? So I went to him.

speaker: interviewee
text: And I say-- his name was Lega. So I told him, Lega, I'm going out. I need some food. The city people are starving. I'm going to collect some money-- some people what I know they're going to give me for free. Some people what I don't know, I'm going to give them. Somebody needs materials, I'll left for him the material.

speaker: interviewer
text: Maybe you can tell me more details like this later. But going to your wife, when, when did you decide to marry her?

speaker: interviewee
text: August, was in 1941-- '41 or '42? Yeah, 1941-- and they took her husband and other Jewish, and 15 people. They took them in the army, in the Russian army. And they took him. And the bombardment was going on terrible. So the Germans bombarded the whole line. Lot of people got killed, most on them in the beginning, actually.

speaker: interviewer
text: So when he was in the army, two guys after two weeks-- two guys ran away. Two guys ran away, two of them. One was in Israeli Jews. He just died. Oh, he died about five years ago. So he came. And another one came. And they said that Schulem, Schulem got killed. And so was [INAUDIBLE], so was [INAUDIBLE], and so has this and that. So they got killed.

speaker: interviewee
text: And we saw that, that it's going to be real bad. We sneaked out. And we were hiding. And that's how came back. So [INAUDIBLE] say, that's an eyewitness say. So later, a couple months later, my now my wife, she say, listen, listen.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK, my husband is dead. I'm afraid. I'm afraid. I have the child. He was-- 1941. This was 1941. He was six years old. He was born '36. It's '37, '38, '39, '40, '41. He was six years old. So he say, I watch him. And I'm afraid. Or I'll get killed and he-- you know? We are not going to be together. Who knows what's happened? So maybe we should go and take a [INAUDIBLE], you know?

speaker: interviewee
text: And I was living by my family's brother. I was at that time. So she say, I'm afraid. Cause if something will happen, we should know that you should take care of my child. Nobody know what's going to happen. I didn't feel like doing it. You know, she was older from me. And you know, and this and that. But anyway, you know, she was very good for everybody and very good person. She was helping so many people. And--

speaker: interviewer
text: How older was she?

speaker: interviewee
text: At that time? That time, in 19--

speaker: interviewer
text: How older than you?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, she was two and a half years older. She was born in 1912. And I was born in 1915-- two and a half years.

speaker: interviewer
text: So when did you marry? What is the date?

speaker: interviewee
text: So I'm married about the end of '41. I married her-- just married. There was no more rabbi. The head the rabbi and the other rabbi, everybody is just hiding or disappeared. Nobody even knows.

speaker: interviewer
text: So now, getting back to her.

speaker: interviewee
text: So this we-- so I was taking a plain--

speaker: interviewer
text: When you were taken--

speaker: interviewee
text: A plain religious man, so he gave us-- he gave us the Jewish marriage.

speaker: interviewer
text: So when you were taken away, you were already married?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. When I was taken away to the hard labor camp, I was married.

speaker: interviewer
text: Well, let's go back to the hard labor camp.

speaker: interviewee
text: Now-- oh, you're going to the hard labor camp. I was talking about--

speaker: interviewer
text: No, you were telling me about when you were taken by the Germans and you were doing hard work.

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, the hard work.

speaker: interviewer
text: The hard work.

speaker: interviewee
text: So I was doing the hard work till 1940, till 1940, till April, 1942.

speaker: interviewer
text: And were you in touch with your wife at the time?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, 1942, they took-- they took me to the hard labor camp. When-- I was in touch. I was in touch when I was in the hard labor camp. But how they take me to the hard labor camp-- well, they took us, I don't know, close to 1,000 people-- young men, older men-- not too older men-- young boys, even kids, like 11, 12 years old. They took us to the city hall. And in the city hall, it was there, all regiment of Gestapo. That was really Gestapo. And they took us. And they let us sit all around, all around, double, triple, just to sit around with our hands in the back and start irrigating the [INAUDIBLE]. And they brought up shovels to build, to dig a hole in the middle. So we start digging a hole very deep. They took one Jew from another city, from-- he was from [INAUDIBLE], a city. They took him. They throw him in the grave, in the place, in the ditch, throw him deep. And then they say, throw some mud on him, everybody with shovels to throw mud on him. Later, they say stop. Everybody has to go in the containers, little containers, cups. They open up the septic tanks. They open up. And you have to go take out all the stuff from the septic tank. Some of them didn't have septic and to take with their hands. They say everybody has to take and go and take and, you know, throwing it at-- on the top of this man when he was in the ditch. It was really horrible, so horrible-- unbelievable. And later, when he was already later throwed back mud on him. And he was already thrown in the mud up to here. They say, go ahead. Pull him out. Pulled him out. And then later say, they throw him back in. Three times, they throw him back and in. And later, it's already in the afternoon. Later in the afternoon, sit down. They didn't give us nothing to eat. The second day, he was laughing and screaming. And the third day, they came with trucks. The big trucks, they came, one Gestapo on one side and one on the other side the truck with whips. And everybody Jew, [SPEAKING GERMAN], on the truck, filled in a truck, locked it in. And everybody, you know, bloody-- some of them, some of them catches, some of them didn't catch, and took us to Lemberg, Janow ghetto. It was a horrible place. Yanov ghetto, they let us in in shacks.

speaker: interviewer
text: How long were you in that hard labor camp?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, later, I was in the hard labor camp.

speaker: interviewer
text: Well, how-- when.

speaker: interviewee
text: I was eight months.

speaker: interviewer
text: In this event that happened, what was the name of the person that was in the mud?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, I don't remember now. I don't remember. It's hard to remember. I know he was the head from the Jewish committee. This I know. How they picked him, I don't have no-- yeah, they know how to pick him. Because when they brought them to Janow ghetto from each state, from each city they know the names from the head from the Jewish, from the Jewish committee.

speaker: interviewer
text: What, what--

speaker: interviewee
text: So they know. So they-- that's what they throw him in.

speaker: interviewer
text: When you're throwing the mud and from the septic tank on him, how-- what thoughts did you have?

speaker: interviewee
text: What do you mean, what thoughts? What kind of thoughts could I have? It didn't help. Good or bad, you have to take it. You can no against them. You can't say, what are-- why are you doing this? So this is the-- it's no-- no explanation, no explanation, other than they put us in the, in the Janow ghetto. And they put us in the shacks.

speaker: interviewer
text: There was all rats, full of rats. You couldn't sleep. They were jumping. They were biting people. It was unbelievable. It was so horrible. And we figure, how long are they going to hold us? They gave us a little soup to eat, you know, somebody with a little kitchen, you know, with kettles, giving a little soup.

speaker: interviewee
text: And this for two, three days-- three days over there. On the third day in the-- in the morning of the third day, they came. This was right by the train. They came with the wagons, with cattle wagons, and packed all in, all packed in the wagons, and brought us to Jaktorow in the late in the afternoon. And we arrived to Jaktorow in the hard labor camp, everybody off the trains and with the-- and with the--

speaker: interviewer
text: How long was the journey? How long was the trip?

speaker: interviewee
text: Magazine-- the journey was a whole day, a whole day journey.

speaker: interviewer
text: Would you describe that further?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, the journey, we stay packed. You can't move. And that's it. Everybody knows-- no sitting, no nothing. They just packed like herring, exactly. It was really bad, the heat and everything.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you get water, food?

speaker: interviewee
text: We didn't get nothing. Oh, they-- they didn't gave us nothing, nothing at all. And when we came there, they let us off. And they opened up the big-- the big gates open up. And everybody, everybody before going into the barracks-- they have already barracks build there. I don't know who build the barracks. They put everybody in lines.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did everybody who left in the cars arrive, everybody who--

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, everybody arrived.

speaker: interviewer
text: Alive?

speaker: interviewee
text: Everybody-- maybe not. You know, I wouldn't even know. [INAUDIBLE] maybe somebody was choked to death, too. Because it happens in other trains. A lot of people, they've packed in so much. A lot of people couldn't take it. And they were choked. In my wagon, nobody, nobody choked. It was--

speaker: interviewer
text: What was the worst feeling about being in there?

speaker: interviewee
text: Feeling? Feeling? There's no, there's no, no [NON-ENGLISH SPEECH]. That's it. You can't-- you can't change. You can't do nothing about it. So when they let us off and put us in lines, and they said, everybody to run. Now that's the place to run. And you have to run. Who was running fast on the right side, who didn't run, who was very weak and sick even from the ride alone and without food couldn't run, on the left side. So from that the whole group-- the whole group was 162 people where they couldn't run. That was on my father's cousin's husband. He was from another city. He was there. And he was put on the side, and also a lot of friends and my wife's oldest brother was there. And he was put into that the first day.

speaker: interviewer
text: So later on, they took them all on trucks in the woods. And with machine guns, they shot them. And other-- they took other guys-- I don't know, they didn't took me-- with shovels to bury them in the woods over there in Jaktorow. And then go to the barracks, you know, wooden barracks-- and everything was wood, laying there. And it go on for eight months. What day did you arrive there?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, what day? It's the same time, 1942-- April, 1942, I arrived there. And then the food was very little. The soup was very bad. We used to get packages. Once in a while, they let in, you know, dry, dry bread my mother used to send me, and also another aunt sent me from [INAUDIBLE]. And then it came, it came to the end. It was going on for a long time.

speaker: interviewer
text: Could you describe--

speaker: interviewee
text: They used to cut-- when somebody was sending bread, they have to cut the bread, see, and see if there's no money. But you can buy nothing for money there. But there's tobacco. And most of the time, they send tobacco. We have a tobacco cigarette, you know? It was smoking was very good. So used to cut it, take out whatever what's in there. And the Ukrainian police-- it was all Ukrainian police-- watching us and doing that.

speaker: interviewer
text: How did they treat you?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, they treat very bad. Oh, the Ukrainians, they were the worst. They were the worst beating ups. And then they started--

speaker: interviewer
text: Could you describe?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, when you stay on the road and you don't do the work, you straighten out, they go right away with the whip and beat you. That's the way it happened. But in the beginning, we have to go in the mountains. They took us with a truck in the mountains, get to the mountains, chop with the hammers from the mountains stones, chop them in small pieces with the hammers, put them on carriages, bring them up on top, put them on the trucks, bring them to the roads-- lined up the whole, how many miles, you know, road. But later they took-- we, we have to take out the old stones. We have to take out. It was very hard to pick out the old stones. Most of them-- a lot of them were also with our transfer, there a lot of from Lemberg ghetto what they put on our transfer-- engineers, doctors. Unbelievable. They could-- they never know this kind of work to do. And they didn't know even how to take the-- this-- the stones out. So I used to go in. I used to show it. If you want to take out the stone, you have to go from the side. Start from the side. And then it'll keep pulling away. When you, when you chip like this, you can't do nothing.

speaker: interviewer
text: So a lot of the Ukrainian police, he say, why you show him? I say, why don't you show him? But he know that I have another job. I get another job. And I was the [NON-ENGLISH]. [NON-ENGLISH]. Means when they finished the job, how much is finished every-- you know? So I go. I had a special bag, a plastic bag with stones with all kind of materials to put in. If it's too high, pull it out, straighten it out. If it's too low, fill it in. That was my job. And they know the meister needed me. They don't have nobody, you know, that know this.

speaker: interviewee
text: Because I was from a village. I used to know everything all around, the works. So I keep, I keep telling them, you know, when the hands swollen up, you keep working who is not used to this kind of work especially. And the hands swollen up over here. That didn't give you no, no, no gloves or whatever. You know, it was just who couldn't work, you stay in the, in the, in [INAUDIBLE]. You stay in the [INAUDIBLE]. And you, you get shot.

speaker: interviewer
text: Now from the beginning, who stays in the [INAUDIBLE]? Five, ten people couldn't go to work. Take them out on the truck in the woods, shot them. And that's it, finished. Did you ever see that?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, I didn't see it. Because they took them out. We know what's, what's happened that they took them out. This was their, their system. Now months later, the commandant's name was Grzymeck, what his name-- very, very terrible person. And his helper was Heinz. He was also-- he was a German, real German. But he was a Volksdeutsche, Heinz. And he was also terrible, terrible person. So he say-- the commandant came. He say, he say, from now on, next week there's no shooting the Jew. [SPEAKING GERMAN].

speaker: interviewer
text: Now, we have to take a pause to change tapes.
